name: Goose
provider: Agentic AI Foundation (Linux Foundation); originally Block
description: Open-source, local-first AI agent written in Rust, created by Block and donated to the Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation (repo moved to aaif-goose/goose, April 2026). Ships as a CLI and desktop app, is MCP-native with 70+ extensions, and works with 15+ LLM providers including fully local models via Ollama. Free under Apache-2.0; vendor-neutral foundation governance is its core trust story, with the usual MCP/prompt-injection surface of local agents.

evidence.value: Executes coding and workflow tasks end-to-end (editing, running, testing); results depend on the user-selected LLM, from frontier APIs to local Ollama models

task_completion_accuracy.methodology: Capability review across coding and automation workflows; model-agnostic design means accuracy tracks the configured model

evidence.value: MCP-native from the ground up with 70+ extensions and 15+ LLM providers across macOS, Linux, and Windows

tool_use_reliability.methodology: Review of MCP extension system maturity and built-in developer tooling reliability

evidence.value: Autonomous multi-step execution with recipes for repeatable workflows and lead/worker multi-model configurations for planning versus execution

multi_step_planning.methodology: Evaluation of recipes, lead/worker mode, and long-horizon task behavior

evidence.value: Session persistence and resume, .goosehints project context files, and an optional memory extension; no managed long-term memory service

memory_persistence.methodology: Review of session storage, hints files, and memory extension capabilities

evidence.value: Agent loop retries failed commands and self-corrects from tool errors; a repetition inspector guards against runaway loops

error_recovery.methodology: Observed recovery behavior from failing commands and the tool-inspection pipeline design

evidence.value: Subagents and recipe-driven task delegation supported; lead/worker model splits planning and execution across models

agent_collaboration.methodology: Testing of subagent delegation and multi-model orchestration features

evidence.value: No OS-level sandbox by default: goose runs shell and file tools with the user's privileges; permission modes (Auto, Approve, Smart Approve, Chat) gate execution rather than isolate it

tool_sandboxing.methodology: Execution isolation review; containerized deployment is possible but user-managed, mitigation is approval-based

evidence.value: Administrators can enforce an extension allowlist (GOOSE_ALLOWLIST) restricting which MCP servers can be installed; tool calls pass a stacked inspection pipeline (security, egress, adversary, permission, repetition) before execution

access_control.methodology: Review of permission modes, allowlist enforcement, and the tool inspection pipeline

evidence.value: Docs warn goose may follow commands embedded in fetched content; inspectors and approval modes reduce blast radius but the MCP/web-content injection surface matches peer local agents

prompt_injection_defense.methodology: Injection surface review across MCP extensions and web-fetching tools; defenses are procedural (approvals) rather than architectural

evidence.value: Local-first: sessions, keys, and files stay on the user's machine; isolation between projects or extensions is not enforced beyond OS permissions

data_isolation.methodology: Data-flow review of local-first architecture and extension access scope

evidence.value: Apache-2.0, fully open Rust codebase (50.9K stars), now under vendor-neutral Linux Foundation AAIF governance with open security advisories

open_source_transparency.methodology: License, source availability, and governance review

evidence.value: Local-first design: no vendor cloud stores prompts or code; session data lives on the user's machine

data_retention.methodology: Privacy architecture review of the self-hosted, local-first model

evidence.value: As self-hosted software, GDPR posture is determined by the deployer and chosen model provider; fully local operation supports strict data-residency requirements

gdpr_compliance.methodology: Compliance capabilities assessment across deployment configurations

evidence.value: Data flows only to the user-configured LLM provider and any installed MCP extensions; telemetry is limited and controllable

third_party_data_sharing.methodology: Data-flow analysis across model providers and extensions

evidence.value: Runs fully offline with local models via Ollama and other local providers; CLI and desktop are both local applications

local_deployment_option.methodology: Deployment options assessment including air-gapped local-model configurations

evidence.value: Comprehensive docs covering permissions, allowlists, extensions, recipes, and enterprise guidance, maintained under the AAIF
Full session transcripts of tool calls and outputs; permission decisions persisted in permission.yaml; observability integrations available but not enterprise-grade by default

execution_traceability.methodology: Review of session logs, permission records, and observability hooks

evidence.value: Approval modes surface each intended tool call with allow/deny prompts before execution, making agent intent visible step by step

decision_explainability.methodology: Assessment of pre-execution visibility and reasoning transparency

evidence.value: Apache-2.0 Rust codebase under Linux Foundation AAIF governance, donated by Block alongside Anthropic's MCP and OpenAI's AGENTS.md at the foundation's launch
AAIF formed 2025-12-09 with goose as an anchor project; platinum members include AWS, Anthropic, Block, Bloomberg, Cloudflare, Google, Microsoft, and OpenAI

open_source_code.methodology: Open source and governance review; repo migration to aaif-goose completed April 2026

evidence.value: 50.9K stars, 5.5K forks, active Discord, and steady releases (v1.41.0 on 2026-07-03)
Repository and docs migrated from block/goose to the Agentic AI Foundation on 2026-04-07, broadening the contributor base beyond Block

community_activity.methodology: Community engagement analysis of stars, release cadence, and post-donation governance

evidence.value: Single-binary CLI install or desktop app on macOS/Linux/Windows; provider setup is a guided config flow, and MCP extensions install in one step

ease_of_integration.methodology: Setup time and integration surface assessment across CLI and desktop

evidence.value: Primarily a single-user local agent; headless/API usage and recipes enable automation, but fleet orchestration is left to the deployer

scalability.methodology: Assessment of headless usage, automation hooks, and organizational deployment patterns

evidence.value: Free Apache-2.0 software; costs limited to the chosen LLM API, and fully local models make zero-marginal-cost operation possible

cost_predictability.methodology: Pricing model analysis of free software plus BYO model costs

evidence.value: Session logs and cost/token tracking built in; centralized monitoring, alerting, and audit dashboards require external tooling

monitoring_capabilities.methodology: Monitoring features assessment for individual and enterprise use

evidence.value: Mature 1.x series (v1.41.0, 2026-07-03) with frequent releases; used internally at Block scale, and foundation governance reduces single-vendor abandonment risk

production_readiness.methodology: Maturity assessment from release history, Block production usage, and governance continuity

code-generation.notes: Strong local coding agent with LSP-free simplicity and MCP extensibility; quality tracks the chosen model

data-analysis.notes: Good for scripted analysis and workflow automation via shell and MCP extensions

research-assistant.notes: Capable with web/MCP extensions; injection caution needed when fetching untrusted content

content-creation.notes: Serviceable for docs and technical writing; not its design center

best_for: Developers wanting a free, open, local-first agent that works with any model including local Ollama
Privacy-sensitive teams needing code and prompts to stay on their own machines
Organizations that value vendor-neutral foundation governance over single-company tools
MCP-heavy workflows leveraging its 70+ extension ecosystem
strengths: Vendor-neutral governance: donated by Block to the Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation (Dec 2025 announcement; repo moved April 2026)
Local-first Rust application: fast, no vendor cloud, works fully offline with local models
MCP-native with 70+ extensions and 15+ LLM providers
Layered controls: permission modes, extension allowlist, and a stacked tool-inspection pipeline
Free Apache-2.0 with an active community (50K+ stars) and steady release cadence
Both CLI and desktop app across macOS, Linux, and Windows
limitations: No OS-level sandbox by default; shell and file tools run with full user privileges
Prompt injection via MCP extensions and fetched web content remains an open risk, mitigated only by approval modes
Single-user focus: no built-in multi-tenant, fleet management, or enterprise audit dashboards
Output quality varies widely with the configured model, especially small local ones
Post-donation transition (repo/docs moves, org rename) creates some ecosystem link rot and tooling churn

metadata.deployment_type: Local CLI and desktop app (macOS, Linux, Windows); self-hosted
metadata.architecture: Local-first Rust agent with MCP-based extension system, permission modes, and recipes
metadata.governance: Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF) at the Linux Foundation since April 2026 (announced 2025-12-09); founded alongside MCP and AGENTS.md
metadata.pricing: Free; users pay only their chosen LLM provider (or nothing with local models)
